items.myth: The gradient always points directly toward the global minimum of the loss.
items.reality: ∇L points toward steepest local ascent of loss; following −∇L is greedy and may wander through saddle points, valleys and multiple minima on non-convex surfaces.
items.example: On f(x, y) = x² − y² near the origin, −∇f = (−2x, 2y) can push toward a saddle, not a unique global minimum.

items.myth: A zero gradient always means you reached a minimum.
items.reality: ∇f = 0 characterizes critical points: minima, maxima and saddles all have zero gradient (when differentiable).
items.example: f(x) = x³ has f′(0) = 0 at an inflection, not a minimum; f(x, y) = x² − y² has ∇f(0,0) = 0 at a saddle.

items.myth: Large gradient norm always means the model is learning well.
items.reality: Large ||∇L|| can signal sharp regions, instability or exploding updates; tiny norms can mean saturation or vanishing signal—context matters.
items.example: A spike in ||∇L|| with η fixed can diverge training; clipping gradients caps the step magnitude regardless of raw norm.
